title: Using a livestock crush safely
subtitle: How to position, use, and maintain a handling crush to keep both the animal and operator safe.
excerpt: A livestock crush allows one person to safely restrain cattle, sheep, or goats for veterinary procedures, foot trimming, and inspection. Using it correctly — correct approach angle, correct head restraint, and correct exit procedure — prevents injury to the animal and the handler. A poorly set-up crush is dangerous for both.
sourceNotes: AHDB, cattle handling and crush safety guidance. HSE, Safe handling of cattle (INDG200). Scottish Government, farm safety handling livestock guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A narrow metal pen with adjustable sides and a headgate that closes to restrain an animal in a standing position. Used for veterinary examination, vaccination, foot trimming, and tagging. Cattle crushes are rated for different maximum weights; never use an undersized crush for heavy cattle.

The front closing section of a livestock crush that captures and holds the animal's head and neck. Two types: self-catching (closes automatically when the animal pushes through) and manual (operator closes a lever). A self-catching gate reduces the handler's workload when working alone.

The area around an animal within which it feels threatened and reacts to a handler's presence. Entering the flight zone from the side or rear causes the animal to move away; from directly behind, it may kick. Understanding flight zones allows a handler to move animals without physical contact.

content.text: is only safe when positioned correctly and used by someone who understands how the animal will behave inside it. Rushing the approach, leaving gaps where a leg can become trapped, or releasing the head before the body is free are common causes of injury.

Site the crush in line with a race or pen so the animal moves naturally forward into it without turning. The floor should be non-slip concrete or compacted hardcore; a sliding animal inside a crush can injure itself or damage the equipment. Make sure the exit gate can open fully without obstruction. Place the crush so you can work on both sides without being pushed against a wall.
Moving the animal into the crush

to encourage forward movement: stand to the side and slightly behind the animal's shoulder. Avoid driving from directly behind. Do not use sticks or electric goads on an animal already in a confined run — it increases panic and the risk of the animal going down.

on a self-catching crush closes when the animal pushes its head through. Stand clear of the front of the crush when the animal is entering; a startled animal can slam its head back hard. Once the head is caught, close the side squeeze bars if fitted to reduce side-to-side movement. Do not over-tighten: the animal should be restrained, not in pain.

Never stand directly behind an animal in a crush without a barrier between you; even restrained cattle can kick backwards. For rear-leg work, stand to the side and use the leg rope to draw the leg clear rather than reaching in behind. Keep children and bystanders back from the crush while it is occupied.

Open the exit gate fully before releasing the head. A partially open gate creates a gap the animal may try to force through and injure itself. Release the head restraint from the side, not from in front. Step clear as the gate opens; the animal will move forward quickly. Never attempt to turn an animal around inside a crush.

Check all pivot pins, latches, and welds for wear after each use. A headgate latch that does not hold under pressure is a hazard. Oil moving parts regularly; a stiff mechanism is slower in use and more likely to fail at a critical moment. Inspect the frame for cracks at welded joints annually.
